description.value: Úbeda is an artistic and monumental city, and one of the most stately and glorious towns in Andalusia. Its most notable splendor took place between the 16th and 17th centuries. The Renaissance strongly marked most of the city's architecture. The many monuments that were built during that time are still buildings that we can continue to enjoy today. The Vázquez de Molina Square is the greatest exponent of this Renaissance display. It constitutes the center of the VUE area (of Exceptional Universal Value), a space that led UNESCO to grant us the title of WORLD HERITAGE CITY in 2003. The spaciousness of the square and the distribution of the many surrounding monuments are not common. Accompanying these monuments are a magnificent Renaissance fountain, and several areas of trees and gardens. Strolling through Úbeda will give us the opportunity to find corners and squares surrounded by magnificent buildings and monuments, which will allow us to greatly enjoy the walk and rest on a terrace while we regain our strength.

name.value: Sanctuary of Our Lady of Guadalupe

description.value: Special protection for an archaeological landmark. Baroque style with a later interior featuring neobaroque altarpieces. Dating back to the 17th century. Chapel dedicated to the Patron Virgin of the city. It has a single nave covered by a barrel vault and a half-orange dome, with a double porticoed gallery. The legend of the origin of the site comes from the story of a farmer who found a small statue there. Some miracles are attributed to this Virgin, such as curing the people of Úbeda from the plague when, on August 4, 1681, the old Hospital de Santiago closed. The Virgin is also the object of worship during the pilgrimage on May 1st of each year.

Archaeological Site PGOU. From the 3rd millennium BC. In the Iberian site of Cabeza Higuerosa, on the old road from Úbeda to Baeza. Megalithic construction of a funerary nature. It is a small stone elevation with a polygonal floor. Excavated in the rock and with a large cover, currently surrounded by native vegetation.

Special protection for architectural landmark. 16th century. It began construction in the year 1562 as part of the improvement and urban reform program of the communication routes that in those days and throughout the entire century continued thanks to the Municipal Council of Úbeda. The bridge stands over the Guadalimar River to cross the Old Road to Toledo (towards La Carolina). It is a 100-meter long engineering piece with a central arch of 36 meters, which is accompanied by four smaller arches on the sides.

BIC (Cultural Heritage Site). 14th-16th centuries. One of the most excellent examples of Andalusian Gothic architecture, and possibly the most orthodox in its style throughout the province. Its main facade, with a Gothic-Flamboyant appearance, was commissioned by the Bishop of Jaén, Alonso Suárez de la Fuente del Sauce. The west facade, on the other hand, is one of the most interesting examples of Mannerism in Andalusian architecture due to its level of experimentation and vigor. Following the project of Andrés de Vandelvira and with the sculpture of Lorenzo Borgio. The bell tower was the tallest in the city until the 19th century, when it was lowered due to its ruinous state. Inside, the Dean Ortega''s chapel and the sacristy (opposite), both built under a program of interventions in the mid-14th century, are outstanding. Its Renaissance design is attributed to Vandelvira. The great ironwork that precedes the chapel is the work of local author Juan Álvarez de Molina.

BIC-Monument. 16th century. A foundational and charitable work, expressly designed for the patients of bubonic plague, a disease that devastated the city at that time. It was endowed with a chapel, giving the building greater symbolic value. Next to it, the burial place of Bishop Don Diego de los Cobos. Both of these elements make the hospital a memorable work, continuing the tradition of building great works in Úbeda. Thus, this building breaks with the usual layout of hospitals and is conceived as a hospital-palace. In this case, Vandelvira culminates in a purification of the prevailing mannerist style, highlighting great stylistic sobriety. In terms of layout, greater importance is given to the chapel due to its location in front of the central courtyard, and to the staircase, one of the most monumental areas. Regarding the chapel, its innovative "H" plan and its roof composed of large vaunted and barrel vaults create a space of great dynamic balance. They are decorated with frescoes. Next to the chapel, the spaces of the sacristy and the antechamber deserve to be mentioned, decorated with frescoes with grotesques and Christian and classical iconography. On the other hand, there is the staircase. A representative place and where the ornamentation is most concentrated. With a double flight and a large landing, the monumental staircase follows the mannerist criteria. The octagonal vault that covers it is lowered, but strategically placed with windows at its start to create a weightless effect in the hours of greatest luminosity. With decoration in coffers and trompe-l'oeil, it attracts the gaze of every visitor.

From the temple built in the 14th century, only its two late Gothic portals have been preserved: the South, in a style similar to those of the churches of San Pablo and San Nicolás de Bari; and the North, which mixes Plateresque decoration. In the mid-16th century, a total renovation of the old medieval church was planned, the influence of Vandelvira is more noticeable in the main chapel and the transept, although much of its material execution was carried out after Vandelvira died in 1575.

name.value: Palace of the Marquis of Mancera

description.value: BIC (Cultural Heritage Site). 16th-17th century. Built at the end of the 16th century and the beginning of the 17th century, it is one of the tower houses that reappear in this period, reintroducing the tower as a representative element of the nobility, reminiscent of the old castle towers, in an urban context where its defensive function no longer has any meaning.

BIC (Cultural Heritage Site) Second half of the 16th century. It is a manor house in which its architect, Andrés de Vandelvira, introduced elements that would later spread to all civil architecture in the following century. This includes the corner balcony, with a central marble column, which is a local variant. The distribution of decorative elements, merely clustered around the main entrance and balconies (leaving the walls bare), and the arcade of the main floor are also characteristics of Vandelvirian architecture and of the Spanish Renaissance that would predominate in the 17th century. It houses one of the most important private collections, as well as the library and the archive.

BIC-Monument. From the 16th century. The temple that was once a symbol and endowed the city with prestige and power, and that today is an emblem for the people of Úbeda along with Santa María and the Hospital de Santiago. The powerful secretary of Charles V, Francisco de los Cobos, spared no expense in creating this great work where he would rest at the end of his life, with the collaboration of eminent authors: Diego de Siloé with the initial designs in 1536, followed by Andrés de Vandelvira in the second phase of 1540. The master Berruguete was in charge of the high altar. The facade and the sacristy are sculpted by the Frenchmen Estefan Jamete and Francisco de Villalpando with the great work of ironwork. It is a building with a great spiritual symbolic load, starting with the floor plan, typical of mathematical humanism where the single nave (of the basilica) and the dome-headed apse (funerary symbol) are combined, being one of the few examples in Spain. It has a highly complex programmatic complexity, with interesting features such as the pagan gods on the intrados of the main door. Esteban Jamete uses iconography from the Old and New Testament as well as from the classical pagan world. Other examples would be the bucrania with garlands on the towers and the reliefs of the labors of Hercules. Also noteworthy is the original entrance to the sacristy, as a display of architectural composition by Vandelvira. El Salvador has a millimetric symmetry and proportion in its decoration and physiognomy, becoming an essential key for the enjoyment and study of the Spanish Renaissance.

The city's largest park, recovered after the land was used as a fairground and remained empty for decades. In 2009, the opening of this large recreational area was inaugurated. It has several different sections for various uses such as a skatepark and courts, a dog park, petanque area, playground, some fountains, walking paths, kiosk, public restrooms, etc. Today, it is an essential spot for those who practice sports daily, due to its great accessibility and welcoming gardens.

It was built in the 13th century, on an ancient Roman road and next to the Almohad wall near the Granada gate. This church underwent various renovations, mixing Romanesque, Gothic, Renaissance, and Baroque styles. At the beginning of the 20th century, it was plundered and closed to worship, until in 2013 it was transferred to the Huerta de San Antonio Foundation, which has been responsible for its rehabilitation and launch as a cultural center.

streetAddress: Calle Miradores de San Lorenzo, s/n.

description.value: 13th-16th centuries. One of the most emblematic towers for the people of Úbeda. Located in one of the main squares and present at many civic events, it also serves as the clock that marks the time for everyone. Originally, it stood defending the busy Toledo Gate within the city walls, which led to the towns of Baeza, Jaén, Toledo, Córdoba, Sevilla, and the court. In the 16th century, the clock was added, adapting the tower''s function. A second body with the machinery was then added, along with an octagonal temple-like bell tower with semicircular arches and interspersed linteled openings, decorated with floral motifs at the corners. At the base, another important element is the Gothic image of Our Lady of Remedies, deeply linked to the city''s history.

Ranked 1st degree in the PEPCH. Dating back to the 15th century. Defensive architecture. It is the only albarrana tower in the walled enclosure. It is solid at its base, and above it houses a room covered with a dome, also with eight sides. A plant typical of the art of the last Muslim period in Andalusia. It retains its original machicolations although it was remodeled in the 15th century. Later, in the 20th century, the immediate stretch of wall was reconstructed, also opening the old gate towards Ventanas street.

Walls and Losal Gate or "Sabiote Gate". BIC-Monument. From the 14th century. Mudejar style. It is one of the most monumental and best-preserved gates of the wall. An example of the forms and construction techniques characteristic of the period after the Christian conquest. It is formed by a double pointed horseshoe arch on octagonal pilasters and above them, a large round arch. It preserves the original beam with the hinges where the door axes were fitted.

name.value: Interpretation Center "The Walls of Úbeda: Tower of the Portillo del Santo Cristo"

description.value: Located in one of the towers of the wall. Inside, visitors will take a chronological tour through the history of the city and its wall, using a multitude of resources, learning about life in a border city and its defensive needs.

The Festival of the Bonfires of San Antón is a highly anticipated tradition in Úbeda, celebrated in mid-January. During this night, considered the longest of the month and a symbol of the beginning of the coldest months of the year, neighbors and visitors gather around bonfires lit in squares and neighborhoods of the city.

The flames, fueled by olive branches and pruning remains, represent purification and protection for the new year. Next to the fire, typical products such as sardines, blood sausage, or popcorn are enjoyed, while reveling in a festive atmosphere and neighborhood camaraderie. Additionally, the blessing of animals is maintained, a gesture that recalls the agricultural origin of the celebration.

Organized by the City Council with the collaboration of associations and local districts, this night of fire and tradition heralds the winter and strengthens the community bonds of the city.

BIC-Monument. It preserves part of the original arch's starting points and the foundations or the stretch of wall where it opened. It gave access to the town of Quesada. In this open space, we find a simple stone fountain with two spouts, at a crossroads to guide us through the Navarro alley to the medieval district of Santo Tomás, and through this reconstructed Mudejar gate, we also access the popular and potter's district of San Millán. From the El Salvador viewpoint, a walk begins along the so-called Mirador Round, where the landscape of orchards and olive groves and the stretches of wall border what was the ancient citadel of the Hispano-Muslim city.

BIC (Cultural Heritage Site) 13th-16th centuries. Among the most important, oldest, and most representative parishes of the city, it holds significant celebratory and assembly importance for the citizens. It retains the original Romanesque basilica layout, although most of the building visible today is in the Gothic style, since it was burned and looted in 1368 during the war of succession. It also includes major subsequent stylistic renovations. The church stands in one of the main and largest squares, namely May 1st Square (commercial in nature), with a staircase leading to a magnificent Gothic-Isabelline portal. Next to it, "el Tabladillo" was added in the 16th century, a Renaissance gallery from where ecclesiastical and Council decrees were read, and from where public events in the square could be observed. Another notable portal for its antiquity is the Carpenters'' Portal, at the foot of the church. It is one of the very few examples of late Romanesque architecture in the city. Another element to highlight would be the Renaissance fountain on the side of the church, built in 1591 as part of a program of reforms and improvements carried out at the time. Inside, over the Gothic setting, the great work of Renaissance ironwork in the noble burial chapels stands out.

Hispano-Muslim style. The Puerta de Granada, where we are now, is so named because it provided access to the old Royal Road to Granada. Next to this gate was the Arch of San Lorenzo, which was demolished in 1856.

This gate, formed by a semicircular arch, is part of the walls of 
Úbeda, a defensive element of military origin. Next to it, there is a 
now-unused watering trough and above it a machicolation that allowed for the launching of projectiles to protect the vulnerable areas of the wall.

Although this walled enclosure dates back to the 9th century, it has undergone 
different modifications throughout history. Originally, according to collected texts, the wall had 13 gates and although it is not known for certain, there are popular sayings that indicate up to 38 towers, guarded by the most important families of Úbeda.

Regarding this gate, tradition has it that Queen Isabel the Catholic passed through it when she was heading to sign the agreement in Baza for the conquest of Granada.

Another legend or popular saying indicates that anyone who can eat a 
pomegranate without dropping a single seed to the ground will find a treasure at the foot of 
this gate.

Public thoroughfare PGOU. 9th-15th centuries. A walk that surrounds what was originally the Hispano-Muslim fortress, which was destroyed in the 16th century by order of the Catholic Monarchs. The route shows, on one side, several sections of Islamic and Christian walls and also towers facing the surrounding sea of olive trees where, from the highest point of the natural spur, the nearest towns can also be seen.

name.value: Pilgrimage of the Virgin of Guadalupe

description.value: On the first of May, the popular pilgrimage of the Virgin of Guadalupe takes place, the patron saint of the town, which brings together horsemen, floats, and pilgrims to the hermitage of Gavellar to accompany her, dressed as a shepherdess, to Úbeda.

Founded by the Franciscans in the 13th century, it is one of the oldest convents in the city. It is famous because Queen Isabel "the Catholic" slept there in 1489, when she was on her way to sign the agreement in Baza for the "Conquest of Granada". From such a distinguished stay, the cell in which the queen slept is still preserved, along with some of her personal items given to the community as a token of gratitude for the hospitality of Úbeda. You can also find its open Nun''s Turnstile, where traditional homemade sweets can be purchased.

Space discovered in 2007 by the Crespo-López family. It is a private initiative to recover a space that was camouflaged within the walls of several buildings, where different families were leading normal lives, unaware of the grandeur hidden within the walls of their homes. Visitors can see the Inquisitor''s room, the courtyard, the cellar, and the ovens. The most unique element is the Mikveh, or ritual bath of purification.

It was founded in the 15th century. Its friars aimed to provide spiritual assistance to the condemned and give them burial, either in the portico of their convent church or under the arcades that existed on the old Calle de la Cárcel. The convent was demolished in the mid-20th century, with only a portal remaining, featuring a semicircular arch flanked by columns topped by a frieze and a niche. It is currently the Francisco de los Cobos high school.

streetAddress: Calle San Juan de la Cruz

description.value: Plaza de Toros or Coso de San Nicasio. Classified as a 2nd degree heritage site in the PEPCH. It dates back to 1857. Its importance lies in being one of the first five bullrings built in Spain. Previously, popular festivities with bullfights were held in the streets and squares until, at some point, they began to use this typology inspired by the Roman amphitheater, including materials such as iron for the arches of the stands, a characteristic of the 19th century. In this case, materials from the old convent of San Nicasio were also reused (the plaza was built in the convent's orchard area), such as the large interior door dating back to the 16th century. Some parts of the convent of San Antonio were also reused. Currently, bullfights are held on the day of San Miguel, coinciding with the fair, until October 4th.

Rated as a 3rd degree heritage site by the PEPCH. Until the late 20th century, all the buildings on this street were pottery workshops, pottery stores, or brickworks for the production of construction materials. Their concentration in this area for centuries is due to a natural factor: the large amount of water that runs through the street's subsoil, an essential element for working with clay. The kilns for firing the pieces were of Arab origin, and the fuels used were olive pomace and firewood. Only one Arab kiln remains on the street, but there are several pottery workshops.

name.value: Viewpoint and monument of Álferez Rojas Navarrete

description.value: Green area according to the General Urban Development Plan. It offers stunning views of the Guadalquivir Valley, surrounded by pine forests and shaped like a small roundabout at the bottom of the Cava gardens. In the center of the park, before reaching the viewpoint, there is a bronze commemorative sculptural monument dedicated to Alférez Rojas Navarrete, who died in the war in Morocco. This work was created by Amadeo Ruiz Olmos in 1960.

BIC (Cultural Heritage Site). 14th-15th centuries. An interesting example of Mudejar-style domestic architecture. A model of Mediterranean house: with a central courtyard and around it, four galleries with different rooms. At the entrance, we find the interesting arcade of the current porch, through which one enters, and which in the past led to the rear part to the courtyard or garden. Today, its orientation is truncated due to the constructions of later attached residences. In 1964, it was rebuilt using the same materials (and others from similar buildings around it) to house the Archaeological Museum with various collections from the municipality and other places in the province. Its layout is divided into: the ground floor with room I of Prehistory. With a repertoire of stone utensils from Paleolithic sites in the province. In the courtyard, sculptural pieces from Old Úbeda. In the other two rooms on the second floor and in the arcades, Mudejar latticework wood, pieces from the Iberian and Roman periods, votive offerings, ceramics, etc., are displayed. Next to the museum is the facade of the now disappeared Casa de las Culebras (end of the 16th century), so named for its decoration.

16th Century. The Palacio de los Granada Venegas is an impressive Renaissance construction dating back to the 16th century. The palace stands out for its imposing facade, featuring a combination of Gothic and Renaissance elements. Inside, one can admire magnificent courtyards, galleries, and halls decorated with exquisite architectural details. The palace currently houses the Renaissance Interpretation Center, where visitors can learn about the history and art of this era in Úbeda.

With its own distinctive and unique personality, Holy Week in Úbeda takes on extraordinary grandeur and emotion. The processional parades of the eighteen brotherhoods, some of which date back to the 16th and 17th centuries, exude unparalleled solemnity and brilliance. It is a complete sensory experience, engaging the entire body in the myriad sensations of Holy Week. Set against the backdrop of medieval and Renaissance Úbeda, the city becomes the beautiful stage for an exquisite artistic representation. Úbeda's Holy Week was declared a 'National Tourist Interest' in 1980.

Ranked as a 2nd degree historical heritage site by the PEPCH. 17th century. It features a facade with a Carmelite style: with a semi-circular arch flanked by pilasters, central niche with pinnacles and shields of the Order on both sides with a triangular pediment. Inside, the church has a Latin cross plan and the courtyard has a double porticoed gallery. To this day, it is inhabited by the Barefoot Carmelite nuns, famous for their pastry work. This Convent was founded on the initiative of Doña Jerónima Enríquez de Carvajal, 'in the houses of her residence which are in the Parish of Santo Tomás', in March 1595; its first Prioress being the Reverend Mother Ana de la Encarnación, a companion of Saint Teresa.

Palace of the Marquises of Bussianos. Classified as a 2nd degree building in the PEPCH. From the 16th century. Typology of palace in urban fortress style, with a project of a lateral tower (unfinished) as an allusion to the warrior past and the thriving nobility that inhabits it. Something typical of this century and an example that we also find in the Palace of the Marquis of Mancera. Belonging to the lineage of the Messía, this family developed a taste for the exotic as reflected in the interior decoration and in their prominent mascarons (Indian type, reminiscent of Olmec heads) on the central balcony of the facade, one of the most interesting points. It is also worth mentioning the interior staircase with its large dome covering it, which is the widest in all the civil architecture of the city.

BIC (Cultural Heritage Site) 16th century. Plateresque style. The commander of the Order of Santiago and city councilor, Don Andrés Dávalos de la Cueva, ordered the construction of this grand palace, making it the first mansion in Úbeda. It combines elements from the medieval period and the early Renaissance, and even includes late Gothic elements. Its impressive facade is flanked by two large towers, symbols of the family''s prestige. From the extensively renovated interior, the courtyard is preserved, where the decoration stands out. Around it are distributed tondos with reliefs of representations of knights and virtues, mixing with wise men or heroes from antiquity and even gods, in a strange and unprecedented conjunction. Another element that attracts attention are the monstrous gargoyles, representative of vice and irrationality in contrast to the virtues depicted in the tondos. This iconic monument is the subject of one of the most well-known local legends: that of the walled-up woman. Currently, it serves as the School of Arts and High School of Arts Education.

Protected area in 2nd degree in the PEPCH. It comprises a stretch of street that borders the city to the south. It connects the church of San Lorenzo with the gardens of La Cava, establishing a dialogue between the landscape and the urban environment and creating an attractive interrelationship between landscape and city. These two environments are separated by a fence that serves as a barrier, an open window overlooking the Guadalquivir, with a splendid panorama of orchards, olive groves, and Sierra Mágina on the horizon.

name.value: Hospital of the Honorable Old People of El Salvador

description.value: BIC (Cultural Heritage Site). 16th century. Built by the charitable organization (Honored and Venerable Elders of El Salvador) as an asylum for incapacitated Christian elderly. Although the former hospital was founded in 1392, it was completely rebuilt in the 16th century. It is attached to the imposing rotunda of the Sacred Chapel of the Savior. With an unfinished appearance, it features a stone facade, a work by Vandelvira in Renaissance style with Serlian motifs and interesting light plays. Of the courtyard, only two wings are preserved, with lower and upper galleries that form one of the most explicitly Mannerist courtyards in the city.

name.value: Convent and Museum of San Juan de la Cruz

description.value: Opened in 1978, it preserves various relics and other mementos related to the Saint. The tour brings you closer to the life and work of the mystic. In addition to a notable library specialized in Sanjuanist themes and spirituality. The Museum currently has twelve rooms where visitors can learn about this great universal figure of thought and Christian spirituality.
Hotel ***

Public Road PGOU. Located in a privileged enclave, at the end of Vázquez de Molina square, in the same direction it evokes and having crossed the area of El Salvador. We find the access point to the Mirador Roundabout at a perpendicular intersection where the mirador wall separates the city from the landscape. A cozy corner like a balcony that overlooks the valley, full of olive trees. On the other side is the Santa Lucía square and the Gradeta de Santo Tomás.

name.value: Church of Saint John the Baptist

description.value: Also known as Cristo Rey or the Jesuit Fathers' Church. Built in neoclassical style, the church is integrated within the Educational Center "Escuelas Profesionales de la Sagrada Familia". The most remarkable feature is clearly its facade, a magnificent work by Francisco Palma Burgos. He began the project at the end of 1952, using limestone for its construction, in an extraordinary relief of over one hundred square meters.

streetAddress:
Calle Baja del Marqués, 4
23400  Úbeda, Jaén  (Andalucía)

description.value: Classified as a 2nd degree historical heritage site. From the first decades of the 16th century. Plateresque style. Currently houses a senior residence. It features an exuberant facade flanked by large columns spanning both floors, adorned with grotesque decorations. Medallions depicting Charity and Virtue, as well as bearded wild men, typical of the style. The heraldry on the wild man's shield has been removed.

name.value: Pottery District and Santo Tomás Route

description.value:
In 2012, Úbeda was declared a ''Zone of Artisanal Interest of Andalusia''. The declaration recognizes the relevance and uniqueness of the city''s crafts, as well as the diversity of existing trades and the artistic quality of the pieces, which combine tradition with new designs.

The surviving artisanal trades take us back to a long historical experience: pottery, artistic forging, cabinetmaking, artistic stained glass, vegetable fibers, and Mudejar coffered ceilings are some of the sectors in which the artisans excel, exporting their production to various parts of the national and international geography. Each of their products enjoys the exclusivity and authenticity characteristic of handmade items, resulting from the transformation of noble materials with traditional techniques. Additionally, they have significant recognitions within the artisanal world (Master Craftsmen, National Craftsmanship Award, National Design Award...).

Any item produced in the workshops of Úbeda, whether large or small, simple or complex, is today a museum piece, worthy of being preserved and admired.

13th-16th centuries. Located outside the city walls, in the so-called potter''s quarter, it was one of the first Christian foundations in our city. Only the tower of the original building has been preserved, as the temple was renovated from the 16th and 17th centuries onwards. Inside, the Virgin of Solitude is venerated, who plays a central role in one of the most spectacular and emotional moments of the Holy Week in Úbeda.

name.value: Basilica of Saint Mary of the Royal Alcazars

description.value: BIC (Cultural Heritage Site). The Basilica of Santa María de los Reales Alcázares, the main church of the city. The Christian church occupies the site of the former main mosque of the Muslim city, and is the result of a very long construction process that spans from the 13th to the 20th centuries. On the main facade, which is late Renaissance like the side one, the relief of the Adoration of the Shepherds stands out, based on a drawing by the Italian Mannerist painter Zuccaro. The two facades give access to a beautiful Gothic cloister from the late 15th and early 16th centuries. The interior of the temple consists of five aisles of equal height, separated by pillars and pointed arches, which open into a very high number of chapels, with some of the most excellent examples of Andalusian religious ironwork. Both spaces, the cloister and the church, correspond to the ablutions courtyard and the interior of the former mosque, to which we are transported by its enormous dimensions and by the location of the pillars in its five aisles.

name.value: Church and Convent of the Most Holy Trinity

description.value: 16th-18th centuries. Baroque. On the exterior, the ornamentation is centered on the facades, with large moldings that are set back and form volutes with representative images of the Holy Trinity and Saint John of Matha. The interior of the church is covered by a barrel vault with lunettes, and a dome with scallops over pendentives, filled with magnificent pictorial and sculptural decoration. The use of Solomonic columns in the interior windows is noteworthy, used here for the first time and representing, once again, a rare example of Baroque. Next to the church is the convent, of which parts of two cloisters are preserved.

San Pedro Church. Classified as a 2nd degree monument in the PEPCH. It dates from the 13th-17th centuries. It holds great historical value as it is one of the oldest parishes in the city, rebuilt in the 14th century, preserving the original polygonal apse. It has undergone several interventions, such as the addition of the Renaissance facade by Vandelvira's disciple, Alonso de Barba, in the year 1605-1605. It is possible that in its origins it was a mosque converted to Christian worship after the conquest. In the mid-19th century, the parish was relegated to Santa María. Inside (rebuilt several times), it has a single nave, a barrel vault, a lowered dome, and side chapels in Gothic style.

13th-16th centuries. Currently the headquarters of the Union of Brotherhoods. Its origin is not precisely known, as we are dealing with a building where different periods and styles coexist. There are preserved medieval proto-Gothic remains typical of fortress-churches on the north facade. The current space, however, was rebuilt in the 15th century in Gothic-Mudejar style. Additionally, its portals are Renaissance, and the southern one is in the Plateresque style. This last one is perhaps the most notable for being the first in its style, and also for its quality. It features the coats of arms of the founding bishop. The tower was rebuilt later in the year 1702. It served as a parish until 1848 when it became the property of the Orozco family. However, it remained open until the year 1936 when it was closed, then being used as a storage for thrones and workshop.

From the 16th century, it is a palatial mansion organized around a central columned courtyard, following the models developed in civil architecture in the city, with the facade divided into two sections. It currently serves as a venue for private events.

name.value: Palace of the Marquess of Contadero

description.value: Classified as 2nd degree in the PEPCH. 18th century. Late Renaissance or "neo-mannerist". Municipal offices. With a classical composition, and with a setback entrance that emphasizes its importance in the square. The open gallery composed of three lowered arches appears in the last section, something very typical of palace architecture in the area.

BIC (Cultural Heritage Site) Built around the middle of the 16th century by Andrés de Vandelvira, one of the finest architects of the Spanish Renaissance. With this palace, Vandelvira achieves the most classic work of his entire career and perhaps of the entire Andalusian Renaissance. Free-standing on all four sides, it is horizontally structured into three floors and vertically into seven differently wide sections, to achieve an optical effect of symmetry and solidity. Moreover, Vandelvira, in a display of freedom, reverses the established architectural orders, placing the Corinthian on the first floor, the Ionic on the second, and on the third floor eight atlantes and caryatids. The interior is organized around an elegant columned courtyard, where the groin vaults and the chromatic contrast between the color of the stone and the white of the lime and marble, relate it to the courtyards of Renaissance Florence.

17th century. Renaissance. It is currently the city''s Superior Conservatory of Music. The building follows the common model of public civil buildings from the Italian Renaissance, featuring a facade with a double porticoed gallery. The upper gallery was used for members of the council to attend events held in the square, and the lower gallery served as a meeting place for citizens, similar to the rest of the square which was also entirely porticoed.

BIC (Cultural Heritage Site). One of the most important archaeological sites in Andalusia, both for the historical significance of its assets, its state of preservation, and, above all, for its research program. Its records, with an uninterrupted occupation of more than 6000 years, not only identify Úbeda as one of the oldest cities in Andalusia but also allow, thanks to their recording and scientific analysis, to explain and exemplify the history of its origin and, from there, that of the City, since today it is the only case capable of showing this process thanks to its uninterrupted development. Making Úbeda the oldest city (scientifically documented) in Western Europe.

This Archaeological Site, Ruins of Santo Tomás, is located in the ruins of what was one of the main churches built in the city during the second half of the 13th century. It was a beautiful temple behind the Salvador, featuring three naves and several side chapels, offered by the most noble families of the place. During the 16th century, the chapel of Our Lady of the Conception or the Immaculate, the original funerary chapel of the Cobos family, was built. The church was looted and burned during the French invasion, and continued to deteriorate during the 19th century.

Well protected as a 2nd degree historical and cultural heritage site from the 16th century, in Renaissance style. It currently houses the office of the State Tax Agency. It was founded by Rodrigo de Benavides, Count of Santisteban del Puerto, who entrusted it to the order of San Francisco de Paula. Since 1936, when it ceased to be a convent, it has had various uses, including its current one.

name.value: Hotel Palace of the Marquis of La Rambla

description.value: Hotel **

Well protected at 4th degree by the PEPCH. It already existed in the 13th century, and in the 15th and 16th centuries it underwent a strong remodeling in Renaissance style. Only a simple facade and the remains inside of the single-nave chapel are preserved. In this convent, where philosophy was studied, the city's archive had been preserved since time immemorial, perhaps since its foundation. Many council meetings were also held there until the 15th century. Since it was auctioned off in 1836, it has been deteriorating until it reached ruin.

The condition of Úbeda as a border town made it a strategic enclave for centuries, where Jews, Muslims, and Christians have left their mark. We invite you to immerse yourself in a fascinating medieval city where the traces of these three cultures intertwine, forming a rich historical, religious, and artistic kaleidoscope.

Green area according to the General Urban Development Plan. "The water from the Saludeja, health leaves" is a local saying that arises from the popular belief that this fountain contained healing waters. It is located at the foot of the wall, set back to make way for the pillar with a single spout, between the street of the same name and right where the Miradores Roundabout begins.

Classified as 1st degree in the PEPCH. 16th century. Originally belonged to the central courtyard of the palace of don Francisco de los Cobos. It was a gift from the Venetian senate to the emperor's secretary. It has relief decoration of frogs, snakes, and lizards on its eight sides of the basin. It resembles the style of classical Rome.

BIC (Cultural Heritage Site). It was built in the 16th century for a community of "recluded" women. From the 18th century, it was used as a prison for clergy, which is why it is known as the "Bishop''s Prison". Only the facade of the original building remains. Currently housing courts, a Bronze Age necropolis (1400-1300 BC) was discovered under its foundations. The artifacts found are displayed in the Archaeological Museum of Úbeda, in Jaén.
Various activities: CONCERTS, BULLFIGHTS, ICE SKATING RINK, ...

BIC (Cultural Heritage Site). It was built in the 16th century as a former agricultural credit institution. It was used as a grain storage facility to provide loans on modest terms to farmers and local residents during times of need. Its facade is made of well-carved ashlar stone and has three floors. It currently houses the National Police Station.
Hotel **

Ranked 1st degree in the PEPCH. 20th century. Located on Trinidad street, next to the Bussianos palace. Designed in 1964 by Alejandro de la Sota Martínez, who is credited with several post offices in Spain. Representative building of the aesthetic of the second half of the 20th century with exposed brick and metal grilles.

Ranked as a 2nd degree heritage site in the PEPCH. From the 16th century. Don Cristóbal Ortega, Councilman of Úbeda and Stable Master of King Felipe II, had his residence in this place, of which only the facade remains. It is composed of the lower section where the large entrance arch is located, and above this, the relief of the family coat of arms supported by wild men and framed by a laurel wreath. The significance of this facade lies precisely in the appearance of these characters, which can also be found in the Casa de los Salvajes or the Casa de las Torres and could allude to the antiquity of the family lineage.

The San Miguel Fair, from September 28th to October 4th, is one of the most important in the province. According to tradition, it dates back to September 29th, 1233, the feast day of the patron saint, when King Ferdinand III the Saint conquered the city. It is a festival marking the transition from summer to autumn, from the end of the harvest to the beginning of the agricultural year. During the fair, bullfights, a prestigious theater season, numerous concerts, and other cultural events and activities are held both within and outside the fairgrounds.

Coinciding with many of these traditional activities, most of the cultural events are programmed and developed, such as: the Biennial of Antique Dealers, the International Music and Dance Festival "City of Úbeda", the International Film Music Festival "City of Úbeda", the Storytelling Marathon "In Úbeda Stories are Told...", the Youth Week, the Agricultural Machinery Fair, the Tapas Fair, the Autumn Theater Showcase, the Early Music Festival of Úbeda and Baeza, the Commemoration of the death of San Juan de la Cruz, and many other events that take place throughout the year.

Listed as a 2nd degree historical heritage site. From the 17th century. Renaissance style. Currently functions as a family residence. It belonged to Don Pedro Mesía, nicknamed 'de los Porceles', from which it gets its name. It has a setback facade to create a front space resembling a small square. Regarding the ornamentation, it features traditional Renaissance or Herrerian elements; the facade displays the sculptures of Apollo and Mercury, which are now deteriorated.
Tourist voucher service.

name.value: Manor house at 18 Cuesta del Losal street

description.value: Green area PGOU. 13th-15th centuries. A unique walled section located between the Losal and Santa Lucía gates, where the wall and nature coexist creating a space for walking, recreation, and as a viewpoint. Historically, the 'Huerto del Carmen' was located in this area, which belonged to the convent of San Miguel, within the city walls. This area was leased by the friars until recent times.

name.value: AUDITORIUM OF THE HOSPITAL OF SANTIAGO

description.value: Auditorium of the Hospital de Santiago, headquarters of the International Music and Dance Festival "Ciudad de Úbeda"

Attached to the convent bearing the same name, this church was built in the 19th century in the Neo-Renaissance style. It consists of three naves with a transept, covered by barrel vaults and a half-orange dome with a large lantern in the transept. In 1627, the oratory that is part of the convent was built to house the tomb of Saint John of the Cross, being the first temple in the Catholic world built in his honor. It includes a small high choir located where the cell in which the Saint died was, and where his relics are preserved.
Tourism in Úbeda and Baeza.

name.value: A.P.I.T. Professional Association of Tourist Guides.

description.value: Interpreters of the Heritage of Jaén Headquarters in Úbeda-Baeza.

name.value: Ruins of the Convent of La Merced

description.value: Dating back to the 16th century, only the remains of a Renaissance door attached to a wall section next to the church of San Millán are left. It was founded in the 13th century by lay knights of the Order of the Mercedarian Redeemers with the mission of ransoming captives during the conquest of Úbeda by Fernando III. After the disentailment of Mendizábal, it was closed along with other ecclesiastical properties in 1836.

Plaza Vázquez de Molina or Santa María. Classified as 1st degree in the PEPCH. Area of ​​Outstanding Universal Value. The emblematic square that does not leave the visitor indifferent, when finding behind the winding streets of medieval layout this large open space, bordered by the imposing buildings of Santa María, the Juan Vázquez de Molina Palace, the Dean Ortega Palace and in the background, dominant, the Sacred Chapel of the Savior. These, together with the landscaped areas, the Renaissance fountain and the esplanades create a unique environment from which the monumentality of a square conceived within a triumphant European renaissance, uniting the religious and the civil, can be appreciated. The Vázquez de Molina square, along with the 1st of May, and the Andalucía (although both of a commercial nature) form the nerve centers of the old city. In a certain sense, it replaces the Alcázar as a center of power, already stripped of its previous functions and contents and which is gradually being abandoned.

Public Space PGOU. Located in the vicinity of Valencia Street, the area par excellence of pottery workshops and the cradle of one of the pillars of local craftsmanship. Until the 1960s, most of the buildings in the area were workshops. They all concentrated there due to the abundance of water springs, an essential element for the clay working process. This also helped to avoid inconveniencing other neighbors with the smoke during firing. Currently, five of those potteries continue their precious work, eager to show themselves to the visitor to demonstrate an important part of our ethnological heritage.

Ranked as a 2nd degree heritage site in the PEPCH. Since medieval times, it was located on the outskirts of the walled enclosure, next to the important Puerta de Toledo. It was used for hosting the market, and to this day it continues to be a commercial square (with a structure of arcades or "portalillos"). It has evolved over time, accumulating buildings of different styles, among which the Clock Tower still stands out. There was also a fountain in the center, now disappeared, about which there is an anecdote related to the residents of Baeza, popularly called the "Cipotes" fountain. Today, the square has adapted to the needs of modern life in the city, being the heart of the modern city, and where three of the main roads in the area converge.
Tourist service.
Olive oil mill. Factory for olive oil production, and retail sale of olive oil and other products.

description.value: Where can I find the El Blanquillo Restaurant and the Las Casas del Cónsul Hotel? It serves as a connection between 1º de Mayo Square and Santa Lucía Square.

description.value: Paddle tennis courts, tennis, basketball...

streetAddress: Calle Cronista Juan de la Torre, s/n

description.value: 2 keys

We are facing the square that best preserves its late medieval urban structure. This square is also known as Paseo del Mercado, since public markets were held here for centuries. It was also the venue for various public, political, economic, and religious events, which translated into commemorative events, council elections, public executions, bullfights, etc.

The commercial area par excellence of the city. Along the two streets, we find clothing and footwear stores, especially, but also opticians, computer shops, some cafes, etc., in a pleasant walk that begins at the Hospital de Santiago and ends at Plaza de Andalucía. Two of the most recurrent meeting points.

Along Calle Real, Úbeda offers us a great variety of tapas bars, restaurants, ice cream shops, and cafés from its beginning to its end, where we can also buy local bakery and pastry products and even crafts. It provides a space for recreation and rest, as well as for walking and access to reach the historic center. From Plaza de Andalucía (the current city's nerve center), it leads us to Plaza Vázquez de Molina, an emblematic place and the heart of Úbeda.
Tourist service.

streetAddress: Calle Llana de San Nicolás, 14 - 2º C

description.value: Municipal swimming pool center. It has two large pools and a children's pool. It also has a bar.

name.value: Tourist Apartments "RINCÓN DEL REGIDOR VITORES BOUTIQUE"

description.value: 1 key
MUNICIPAL SPORTS CENTER. Indoor pavilion and outdoor courts (tennis, paddle tennis, basketball, indoor soccer...)
2 keys
2 keys

name.value: Reproduction of Atlante and Caryatid from the Vázquez de Molina Palace

description.value:
In Renaissance style, this intervention is a full-scale reproduction of an atlas and a caryatid from the Vázquez de Molina Palace in Úbeda, one of the architectural gems of the city. The initiative was born with the purpose of bringing these unique figures closer to the public, allowing for a better understanding of Úbeda''s heritage and artistic richness.

The original sculptures are located on the top floor of the main facade of the Vázquez de Molina Palace, 16th century, a work by the famous architect Andrés de Vandelvira, one of the great masters of the Andalusian Renaissance, and situated in the square of the same name. In total, the facade houses twelve figures (six atlantes and six caryatids), all different from each other. The atlantes, male representations, and the caryatids, female, are anthropomorphic figures that serve an architectural function by acting as supporting elements. Both figures symbolize the strength, harmony, and nobility characteristic of Renaissance architecture.

The facade of the palace is organized into four levels that combine different architectural orders: Corinthian on the first level, Ionic on the second, and, crowning the ensemble, the atlantes and caryatids bearing the family shields. Particularly striking is the inversion of the usual classical order—Doric, Ionic, and Corinthian—a detail that adds uniqueness to the building. An elegant projecting cornice tops the construction, while two small towers at the corners add volume and depth, completing a facade that invites one to stop and admire each of its details.

This route begins at Plaza Vázquez de Molina. This square constitutes the most important monumental complex of the city and corresponds to the so-called Exceptional Universal Value, EUV, for which UNESCO declared the city of Úbeda a World Heritage Site on July 3, 2003. Made up of a group of civil and religious buildings constructed in just fifty years, it forms the great Renaissance architectural ensemble of Spain and one of the most important in Europe.

Well protected under grade 3 by the PEPCH. 19th century. Neoclassical. Currently the Tourist Office. A two-part building attached to the Clock Tower on its right side, and to the Arab wall at its back. The building was constructed to house the butcher shops since the market was on the street where it has its entrance, Rastro Street. It served as the headquarters for the National Police Station and later the Local Police, and in 2014 it was readapted to become the Tourist Office.
A space dedicated to exploring the Jewish culture and tradition in our city, through documents, paintings, objects, and utensils.

Classified as a 3rd degree historical heritage site. 19th century. Modernist nineteenth-century work. It brings a romantic image to the Plaza de San Pedro, far from the predominant Renaissance architecture. It stands out for its floral forms and the use of bichromy on the facade. Inside, it features the typical layout with a porticoed courtyard, this time with small columns and cast iron arches, as dictated by the era. It was key in inspiring the work "Beatus Ille" by our beloved author Antonio Muñoz Molina. Currently, the palace is empty.

name.value: Hotel Palacio de Úbeda G.L. Restaurant "El Ábside"

description.value: Hotel *****
Olive oil mill. Factory for olive oil. Sales point at 57 Avda. de Cristo Rey.

H **** BIC (Cultural Heritage Site) Built by Andrés de Vandelvira in the mid-16th century. Its facade features a studied symmetry in the rhythm and size of the openings, and peculiar balconies on the corners. In the courtyard, as in the rest of the city''s palaces, the columns are extraordinarily slender, a characteristic of Andalusian Renaissance, which borrowed from Granada''s Nasrid art.

16th century. It is a manor house commissioned by Doctor Anguís Medinilla. It is another example of a manorial house, featuring a columned courtyard with corbels, as is common in these constructions. The facade, divided into two sections, features a linteled doorway over which there is a balcony with heraldry on the sides and atop pilasters a split pediment with a shield inside. On both sides of the balcony, there are also respective openings topped by a pediment on brackets.

Ranked as a 2nd degree heritage site in the PEPCH. Late 15th century. Gothic-Mudejar tradition. This is a family residence located in the square of the same name, where Gonzalo de Carvajal and his wife, doña María de Peralta, resided. Gonzalo was a descendant of the famous Carvajal brothers who were thrown from the Peña de Martos by order of Fernando IV. It is a simple construction, which is also reflected on the austere facade, as the few openings are distributed in a purely functional manner.
Tourist service.

In June, the Renaissance Festival is celebrated: For a week, the city transforms to recover its history. It dresses up to be the stage for a series of activities that will make you relive the splendor of 16th century Úbeda: theater and dance, exhibitions and conferences, concerts and period dinners...

Classified as a 2nd degree historical heritage site. Dating back to the 16th century, it was the residence of the secretary of Emperor Charles V, a nobleman who rose from a young age to acquire maximum responsibility in 16th century Spain. The current building originates from the expansion of the previous century's family house. After suffering a serious fire, its grand facade remained standing. It stands out for its sobriety, featuring only heraldic elements (similar to those on the capitals of the interior gallery) such as the cross and shells, symbols of the Order of Santiago to which Francisco de los Cobos belonged. Opposite the palace, in the alley, is the building that in 1808 was the Casa Cuna, an institution responsible for taking in abandoned children. They were placed in a turnstile next to the door to protect them inside. The gap where the turnstile was located is still preserved to this day.

name.value: Los Portalillos Pastry and Coffee Shop

description.value: Cafeteria with a variety of pastries for sale (cakes, croissants, donuts, local products, etc).

streetAddress: Cronista Juan de la Torre, s/n.

name.value: Olive Oil and Olive Grove Interpretation Center

description.value:
"TERCIA HOUSE"
Listed in grade 3 by the PEPCH. S. XVI. Current Olive Oil and Olive Grove Interpretation Center. Right next to the octagonal tower. It is a very simple two-story building, which only has coffered decoration on the front. Its name comes from the use for which it was built, since a third of the ecclesiastical tax was collected there. Currently its main motive is the precious olive oil. Inherited from the most remote antiquity, it is a huge part of our gastronomy and the basis for any traditional cuisine. Virgin olive oil is the only one that can be classified as a natural product, since (unlike others) it lacks chemical processes. It is a natural fruit juice that preserves its smell, flavor, aroma and vitamin content. In the surroundings of La Loma the variety of Picual olives is offered, with a stronger and fruity flavor, which does not leave its taster indifferent.

SCHEDULE:

MORNINGS:
FROM TUESDAY TO SUNDAY FROM 10:00 AM TO 2:00 PM

AFTERNOON:
FRIDAY AND SATURDAY FROM 6:00 PM TO 8:30 PM

name.value: Palace of Don Luis de la Cueva

description.value: 15th-17th centuries. This is a manor house that served as the residence of the Cueva family. It consists of two floors surrounding a central courtyard, with some Mudejar elements. On the exterior, the continuous balcony and the entrance arch were remodeled in the 18th century. It is said that King Henry IV stayed there, who favored the nobleman from Ubeda, Beltrán de la Cueva, who was later alleged to be the father of Princess Juana "la Beltraneja". During the Civil War, it was used as a relief house and prison, and later as a carpentry workshop. After its restoration, it currently serves as municipal offices.

Listed as a 4th degree monument in the PEPCH. From the early 16th century. Plateresque style. Current building of the UNED headquarters. In the same line as the first Renaissance, where we also find the palace of the Casa de las Torres. Through a great ornamentation concentrated on the facade, the viewer is indicated the importance and power of the resident family at that time. Above the semi-circular arch door, remnants of the Gothic style can be found, such as the conopial-shaped molding. Further up, flanked by two columns and a balustrade, a double-arched window crowned by scallop shells and a frieze with mascarons and rosettes.

Classified as a 4th degree historical heritage site. Built at the beginning of the 17th century. It features a classicist facade with a large semicircular pediment, adorned with reliefs of angels holding the family coat of arms.

name.value: Jewish Houses on Gradeta de Santo Tomás Street

description.value: Listed as grade 4 by the PEPCH, these two buildings constitute an exceptional testimony of the housing typology that the Jewish community who inhabited Úbeda since medieval times occupied. Converted Jews lived in these buildings between the 15th and 16th centuries, as evidenced by the motifs carved in stone on both doorways. The most repeated motifs are the Star of David or magical symbol of Solomon, formed by the union of two triangles.

Classified as 3rd degree in the PEPCH. 20th century. Modernist. The market is built on the ancient foundations of what was the disappeared convent of La Coronada, belonging to the 16th century. In the adjacent streets and surroundings, there are plenty of simple stone houses with lintel doorways, most of them bearing the Trinitarian cross.

Ranked 2nd degree by the PEPCH. 17th century. Residence with a peculiar facade decorated in the Italian style with coffered panels and semicircular glazed ceramic appliques in blue. Two tenantes hold the coat of arms of D. Gil Ortega y Sotomayor, resident of the Palace and canon of Santa María. On both sides of the coat of arms, two reliefs represent the seal of the Basilica of Santa María. One of these reliefs is of Ntra. Sra. de la Asunción, and the other of San Julián, the canon's patron saint and Bishop of Cuenca, who had the habit of making wicker baskets in his spare time. Hence the name of the House.

The Paco Tito Pottery Museum, Memory of the Everyday, combines some of the emblematic works of this pottery dynasty, with objects from the history of pottery in our city. In the same location, we can visit the pottery workshop and buy some of the unique pieces that are made there.

The origin of this mansion dates back to the mid-13th century. The current house, a 15th-century foundation of the Chirino Narváez family, descendants of converted Jews, retains four 14th-century Gothic-Mudejar octagonal columns from an earlier construction. Inside, there is an interesting exhibition of Andalusian Art motifs. The layout of the house follows the style of the palaces of Úbeda, with a columned courtyard. Noteworthy are its polychrome medieval coffered ceiling and its cellars with barrel vaults. Additionally, it has a rear courtyard that houses archaeological pieces and a Mudejar facade of great interest.

A pottery where you can admire the ethnological heritage that keeps alive a tradition of ancestral techniques for the production of the popular ceramics from Úbeda. Juan Tito (Juan Martínez Villacañas) is a potter from a family of the same guild. Together with his brother, Paco Tito, they are a high exponent in this craft. The imprint of the Mudéjar origin is reflected in the production procedures.

Inside the establishment with a café-bar called Cocolet. There, the little ones can enjoy a pleasant playtime while the adults relax by having a coffee or a snack.

Small enclosure incorporated into the square with games to provide children with the option of a fun place while visiting one of the most emblematic places in the city, or having a drink at the nearest terraces.

In front of the Huertos del Carmen, a walled area that leads to the Puerta de Santa Lucía, there is a small playground with gardens where you can take a break with the little ones in the lookout area.

name.value: Ronda Antonio Muñoz Molina Children's Park

description.value: Recreational area fenced for the little ones, and some other games and swings around. Located in the Miradores Roundabout, with privileged views and close to some cafes and pubs.

This is one of the most important cultural assets of the city today, and since its construction in 1926, it has become a current example of the ancient theaters from the 16th to the 19th centuries. The best theater companies in Spain have performed on its stage, providing the city with a cultural activities agenda every season.
In a secluded space, bordered by a fence at different heights, with an area with games and booths to play. When the weather is good, they also set up terraces to have a drink.
Landscaped area with different games for both children and adults to have a pleasant leisure and rest at the end of our route.
A fun game suitable for all ages that consists of solving mysteries and clues found by the players in order to escape the room within 60 minutes.
Beautiful garden area with playgrounds, petanque games, benches, the monument of Alférez Rojas Navarrete, and in the background a viewpoint.

One of the streets conceived as one of the most commercial since its formation. Along the slope of La Trinidad, we find different establishments such as clothing stores and pastry shops or bakeries with local products.

One of the most powerful commercial areas at the moment. Full of shops, it leads to the Carrefour shopping center, where the movie theaters are also located. It also offers a variety of tapas bars, restaurants, and cafes.
Next to Ramón y Cajal Avenue, with a wide variety of commercial establishments of different kinds.
In continuation with Avenida de la Libertad, it also offers a diverse range of shops, including textiles, technology, food, stationery, orthopedics, and more.
Hypermarket with commercial hours from 9:00 to 22:00, movie theater, clothing stores, electronics, household goods, etc.
With good weather, you can enjoy a pleasant terrace space in the square to have a drink, also trying some of our gastronomy and culture.

name.value: Tapas area at the Town Hall Square

description.value: In front of the City Hall, or the Palace of the Chains, we find several options to have a drink in a pleasant place, with terraces and recreational space for the younger ones.

name.value: Taperías area, Lower Street of El Salvador

description.value: In a privileged setting, between Plaza Vázquez de Molina and the Santa Lucía Viewpoints. Here we find some bars and pubs with terraces to have a drink.

name.value: Taperías area on Cristo Rey Avenue

description.value: Visit a tapas bar to try some of our gastronomy and experience the culture, as well as to have a pleasant time enjoying some drinks.
Understand the spaces drawn between the Church of San Lorenzo and the House of the Towers, also within these limits is the famous Puerta de Granada.
Small semicircular elevated square in front of the facade of Santo Domingo church.

This cozy square brings together the buildings of the Church of San Pedro on one side, the Palace of the Orozco family in front, and on the other side, Santa Clara. A little further ahead, in the vicinity of the church, we also find the Palace of the Counts of Guadiana.
Square framed by the Convent of Santa Clara and the Anguís de Medinilla Palace.

Extraordinary square located at the rear entrance to the Palace of Francisco de los Cobos, with wonderful views of the sea of olive trees that beautify the city of Úbeda. In the center of its charming esplanade stands a wrought iron lantern and a simple stone fountain formed by two spouts.
Sale of local products; bakery, pastry, preserves, etc.
Sale of traditional sweets made with dried fruits and other desserts.
Cafeteria and sale of a wide variety of sweets: from the most typical desserts to the most delicious and innovative cakes for those who want to try something new.
Whether shopping or strolling along the bustling Obispo Cobos street, you will be drawn to the delicious smell of this pastry shop, bakery, and local products store.
Sale of pastries as well as other local products typical of Úbeda.
Sale of typical bakery and pastry products from Úbeda.
Typical and main street of the city. Food shops and others. Until the early years of the 20th century, the food market or flea market was located on this street, hence its name.
One of the most typical and traditional in the city. Recently reopened, it is causing a sensation among visitors and locals alike. The products are homemade and always innovative.
Health center. Outpatient and emergency care.
Civil Guard Barracks. (Former Civil Guard Academy).

description.value: 3 keys

name.value: Tower of the Palace of the Counts of Guadiana

description.value: -The Tower of the Palace of the Counts of Guadiana is one of the most beautiful towers in Spain. A quintessential noble symbol in the civil palace architecture of the city of Úbeda, it displays its ostentatious character and the exaltation of a lineage. In the Mannerist style, it dates from the 17th century. The tower consists of four sections, combining traditional elements of the area with others of Flemish origin and based on sheets of buildings that were widespread at that time. It currently operates as the only 5-star hotel in the entire province of Jaén.
Dog bathing center.
Pet hygiene center.

- Well protected at 4th degree by the PEPCH. 19th century. Eclectic style. It is currently the branch of a banking institution. The building consists of two well-differentiated floors. On the triangular pediment of the central balcony, we can see the Maltese Cross, a symbol that represented the hospitaller order of Saint John of Jerusalem, originally of a benevolent nature.

Ranked 4th grade by the PEPCH. 16th century. Residence of Don Francisco de Vago, butler and administrator in the Úbeda of Bishop Alonso Suárez de la Fuente del Sauce. The name it receives is due to the two wild figures that appear on the facade of the house, holding the family coat of arms with laurels. Like the rest of the examples that can be found in the city (such as on the facade of the house of the Caballerizo Ortega), it is believed that they represent the antiquity of the lineage of these families.

Ranked 4th grade by the PEPCH. 16th century. Ancestral home. Located on Corazón de Jesús street, within the small square of the same name, this house in ancestral style stands. It may have belonged in the past to a part of the Santo Domingo Hospital, being later reformed as a residence. Now it is used as municipal offices.

Casa de los Morales or "del ahorcado". Classified as a 2nd degree heritage site by the PEPCH. 17th century. Renaissance style. Currently houses social housing. A simple construction, an example of the abundant houses of the 16th century, which extend into the following century. However, it is more attractive due to the evocation of the dramatic history that surrounds it. Origin of many literary works from the 19th century. It is said that a knight was punished with hanging there, directly by King Pedro I, for kidnapping a maiden who had already been promised to Pedro Gil. It is also said that it could have been the seat of the Holy Inquisition.
